sentences.text: My name is Leo Berkenwald, L-E-O B-E-R-K-E-N-W-A-L-D.
No.
Well, my Hebrew name was Chaim Lajb Berkenwald.
I was born in Lodz, Poland.
I was born on August 31, 1923.
72 is right.
Well, I was the second child of a family of four.
My father was a plumber, a European type of plumber.
And I-- as far as I can say, I had a normal life.
I attended seven public school classes.
I graduated from public school in 1937.
For about a year or so, I was an apprentice in a tailoring place.
Tailoring.
And I worked in this place till about the outbreak of the war.
As soon as the war broke out, I stopped working in that place.
No, I was not prohibited from going for continuing the education.
But the way it was situated in our standard of living, we had no means of pursuing a higher education.
And the normal trend in our class of people was that after completing the seven classes of public school, the child will pursue in a trade of any kind of-- this type of life.
Before the war, the general population of Lodz consisted of about three-quarters of a million people.
And one third of it, or a quarter of a million was the Jewish population.
It was the second largest Jewish population in Poland.
After Warsaw.
I had a sister one year older than me.
I had a brother two years younger than me, and another sister about five years younger than me.
Well, when the-- when the war broke out, the first few weeks I would say there were about as normal as it could be imagined under those circumstances.
But right after, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Germans gave out all sorts of orders, especially for the Jews, that they were curfewed.
From 7 o'clock-- from 5 o'clock in the afternoon until 7 o'clock in the morning, they were not allowed to go in the-- to be in the streets.
So naturally, this was-- in those days it looked to us that it was a big inconvenience for us.
Not realizing, of course, what was awaiting us later on.
But soon we started to learn when the orders came, for instance, that we are not to leave the houses after 5 o'clock.
And in order to enforce these kind of laws, they would kill Jews in the streets, not only because they didn't obey the order, only just to show that they mean business.

And to make sure that this will influence the others in case they intend to do something which would look to the Germans that they are breaking or they are not obeying the laws that they are telling us to obey.
Yes, exactly.
To set an example for others.
Soon after that, one order came after the other.
And each order was accompanied by a sentence that ended 'whoever will disobey will be shot.' And they started to ask, especially the Jews, to give up valuable things.
They started out with pots and pans, with copper.
Then they continued with silverware, any kind of silver pieces.
And they ended with jewelry, watches, and diamonds, and so on.
They enforced it by going into the houses and search the houses.
And they will drag out people if they found anything in the house.

And they would punish them in a way that the rest of the population had to watch it to see what was being done to these people who disobeyed these orders, in order to make sure that they will feel that in order it should not happen to us, we may as well give it up.
Because the-- the normal consensus was that, sooner or later, if I-- if I hide something, they will find it.
No.
This was before the ghetto.
It wasn't-- they didn't even talk about the ghetto.

But soon after, they started to send in the Jews that were living in the-- in the main quarters of the city, in the more affluent quarters, to the more-- to the slum areas of the-- of the city of Lodz.
And then they officially designated the poorest area of Lodz, that this will be the area of the-- of a ghetto.
And they set the date, which I don't-- I'm sorry that I don't remember the exact date, but it was either the 1st or the 15th of April of 1940.
And this was the time that this ghetto will be sealed.
And by that time, every Jew living in the city of Lodz had to be moved into this area.
And whoever will be caught outside this designated area will be shot.

So naturally, all the people, all the Jews living outside this area, started to move in with as-- as little or as much as they could barely take with them, not even knowing where they will live, in which house and which apartment.

Well, as it was set up, if I remember correctly, since this area that was designated for the ghetto also contained the Gentile population before, the Gentiles had to move out in order to make room for the Jews to come in.

But since it was a small area, more or less, I would say about 12 square kilometers, not that many Gentiles lived in this area to make room for all these hundreds of thousands of Jews that would move in.
Because the-- when the ghetto was sealed finally, there were about 200,000 Jews living in that area.
In other words, in an area of about 12 square kilometers.
For instance, our family of six people lived in two rooms.
And this was considered, in those days, a-- a normal area of living for a family of six.
My father's brother and his family, another family of six, came in to live with us.
So for the-- I would say about two years or so, 12 people lived in these two rooms.
This was-- yes.
It happened that we lived-- what-- it happened that our family lived in an area what-- that was in the ghetto, so that we did not have to move out of it.
But the family of my uncle came in to live with us.
It was a working class people, a-- a-- a-- primarily a working class people area around there.
It was also a-- a slum area.
Part of it, yes.
No, it just happened that our-- our area was considered more like the rest of the city.
But it was designated as part of the ghetto eventually.
Yes.
This is correct.
When the ghetto was organized, everybody-- everybody got a card that allowed him to work.
Everybody was forced to work.
And everyone was assigned in places according to their trades, according to their education.
And everybody, the whole population of the ghetto, starting with small children, had to have a certificate that they are employed.
Because the whole ghetto was converted into a big camp, so to speak, to work for the German armed forces, for the German war effort.

From making uniforms, from electrical work, from making part of ammunition, from making straw boots for the German soldiers later on when they-- when they fought on the Russian territory, especially in the wintertime.
Every conceivable trade was in the ghetto.
And everybody, whatever it was produced, was produced for the German army.
Even the children.
8, 10-year-old children had to work.
Naturally, they did work according to their capacity, according to their--
Ability.
Sweeping, cleaning, picking up things in the factories, which were called in those days resorts.
And they tried to do as much whatever they were told to do.
All these places were in the confines of the ghetto.
We were not allowed to leave the ghetto under penalty of death.
It started in-- it started in April of 1940, yes.
This process went on until the very end, until the ghetto was liquidated four years later.
Throughout--
In August of 1944.
As far as-- yes.
As far as I know, yeah, this was the last ghetto in Poland that was liquidated.
No.
No, just opposite.

When the war broke out, and-- people from Lodz, especially the Jewish population from Lodz, whoever had the means, started to move eastward, trying to get away, or hoping to get away, from the Russian occupation.
Excuse me, German.
They-- they were trying to get away from the-- I mean, from the German.
I'm sorry-- from the-- from the German occupation, towards the Russian borders.
By the time the ghetto was established and closed, about 50,000 Jews or so left Lodz.
And when the Jews had to go in-- into the ghetto, there were about 200,000 of them.
But no-- none Jews from the outlying areas of Lodz came in to Lodz, except that later on when the ghetto was established.
When the ghetto-- when the ghetto was established, from 1940 until 1944, periodically the small towns around Lodz were liquidated.
In the beginning, there were ghettos established in those areas.
And little by little, the Jews were evacuated from these.
These ghettos were liquidated.
And those were-- that were-- those Jews in these areas around Lodz that were capable of work were shipped in-- into the Lodz ghetto.
And the elderly people, sick people and children were sent to Chelmno to be executed.
This way, the population in the Lodz ghetto remained at about the same level.
Because a lot of Jews living in the ghetto died out, because of hunger and other conditions, epidemics, sicknesses, and so on.
So they were replaced by the Jews that came in from the adjacent areas.
The same.

Except also for the fact that during those four years, from time to time, orders came in from the German authority that they need a certain amount of people to be sent out from the ghetto, supposedly for work, whether in other areas in Poland or in Germany.
So orders came in.
They need 2,000 people, people capable to work and with promises of better conditions.
And since the conditions were very bad in the ghetto, many Jews volunteered to leave the ghetto and to go to work someplace else, hoping that the conditions there will be better than the ghetto.
Labor camps.
And these orders came in from time to time.
And the more they came in, the higher was the amount of the request.
From 1,000 people, they moved up to 1,500, to 2,000, to 5,000, and so on.
But then it came a time when rumors started to come in to the ghetto.
As far as I know, I have no idea how they came in.

But they did come in, letting know the-- the Jews in the ghetto that in many cases the Jews that are being shipped out from the ghetto are not going to work that they were promised to, but they went to extermination camps.
We didn't know about Auschwitz in those days, and we didn't know about Chelmno in those days, because the Lodz ghetto was extremely sealed.
In the beginning, we had newspapers come in.
We had clandestine radios.
But those that were caught with these things were shot on the spot or hanged, and the people had to watch them.
So little by little, all these information means disappeared.
And we didn't have any knowledge whatsoever what's going on outside the ghetto, or in the world, or how the-- the war was progressing, and so on.
We had no way of having any kind of information.
And as opposed to different ghettos like Bialystok, or Warsaw, or any other ghettos, we had no contact whatsoever with the outside Gentile population.
They did not help us in any way.
We have no contact with them.
And we did not have any communication whatsoever.
So we were completely ignorant, not knowing at all what is going on.

But somehow here and there, whether a postcard, or a mark, any kind of sign would come in telling us that these people that supposedly went out from the ghetto to labor camps and to better conditions did not reach to these camps.
Instead, they went to extermination camps.

So then, when orders came in from the German authorities that they are looking, or they are asking for people to be sent out to work, fewer and fewer people would volunteer to these-- for these shipments.
So they started to come in-- the Germans came in themselves and grab people for these-- for these purposes.

Another occurrence was from time to time that the Germans applied pressure on the Judenrat, which is the self-government of the ghetto, which consisted of a-- of a leader by the name of Rumkowski with a mini cabinet, so to speak, which each member of this-- of this cabinet had a certain-- he was a head of a certain department.
And the German-- the German authority ordered this Rumkowski, the leader of the ghetto, to supply Jews to be shipped out from the ghetto.
And he, in a sense, was in a bind, because he requested the Jews to go voluntarily.

And since they refused, or the quota was not fulfilled according to the demand of the German authority, he was forced to use the Jewish police to take Jews from the houses and to fill in the amount of people that was needed.

Naturally, this created a resentment from the-- from the general Jewish population towards the establishment of this Judenrat, because he was-- he was accused of working together with the Germans against the Jewish population.
In a sense, it was a fact that he did.

But at the same time, nowadays, I mean, now, in hindsight, you have a better way of reflecting on these days to realize and to understand his condition as opposed to the general opinion of the population of the ghetto.
And it was still a large controversy going on.
And it is very difficult to actually-- to understand and to realize whose fault it was, and who was right, and who was wrong under these circumstances.
In essence, he had no choice.
His argument was-- his argument was, and it was a fact, that if the Jewish police, under his orders, came into the Jewish houses and took out the Jews, he took them out.
I mean, the police took them out.
If the German SS came in to took-- to take out the Jews from the houses, they were tortured.
They would kill them.
If somebody was hiding, they would be shot on the spot.

So the reasoning and the argument of the leader, Rumkowski, of the ghetto, was that if I am doing it, which I am forced to do it, I am doing something that it will be done anyway with less casualties.
My personal opinion, it is difficult to say.

Judging from-- I mean, today, by reading and by listening to people who lived through these-- these times in different areas, I would say that Rumkowski is supposed to take the example of, let's say, the same leader of the Warsaw ghetto and from other ghettos.
That they committed suicide in order not to have on-- on their conscience that they sent Jews to death camps.
We-- we thought, and we were sure at that time, that he knew exactly where the Jews are being sent.
And it was not his right to do it.
And he should have commit suicide just like the others.
So naturally, who am I to say to a man that he should do it?
